titleEn: Never Have We Known Such Yearning

verses.sadr: إن يكن رث من أناس بهم كا
هم أماتوا صبري وهم فرقوا نفـ

verses.ajuz: وهي لا عقد ودها ساعة البي
رميت من أبي سعيد صفاة الـ
سوق موت طمت على كل سوق

verses.sadr: واجدا بالخليج ما لم يجد قط
ولو أن الجياد لم تعصه كا
أن لو أن الذراع شدت قواها
ما رأى قفلها كما زعموا قف
غير ضنك الضلوع في ساعة الرو

verses.ajuz: ي إذا قل ثم هدر الفنيق
من متاع الملك الذي يمتع العي

verses.sadr: ن به ثم من رقيق الرقيق
verses.ajuz: لم تبعهم منهم كبارا ولا صد
فرق ما بينهم وبين ذوي الإش
أي شيء إلا الأماني بين ال
يوم حلق اللمات ذاك وهذا ال

verses.sadr: يوم في الروم يوم حلق الحلوق

verses.ajuz: كم أفاءت من أرض قر من قر
ثم أبت وأنت خوف الغمام ال

verses.sadr: لم تخوف ضر العدو ولا بغ

verses.ajuz: لا يجوز الأمور صفحا ولا ير
أنا ولهان في ودادك ما عش
راحتي في الثناء ما بقيت لي

translationEn: Never have we known such weeping of the yearning soul — how could it be otherwise when tears are the very sign of love? Spare your reproach, for it is a painful thing that one's companion should prove no companion at all. Ask the eyelids to spare their pearl-like tears, for a tear of separation is not lightly mingled with others. Cursed is he who wrongs his parents, and cursed too is he who wrongs a dwelling-place at al-Aqiq. Let the camels halt, casting off their burdens at the beautiful encampment, the abode of the beloved. Perhaps they have passed, those with whom my longing could be healed and my breath eased. They have killed my patience and dispersed my very soul — in their tents is a feast for those who would make the pilgrimage. Supple as a willow branch, with a back like a willow wand and fresh as morning; hers is not a love-bond sealed by the hour of departure, nor is her waist bound tightly by any belt. It is as if the red wine courses through the water of her cheeks together with the redness of al-Aqiq. She is like a gazelle in bloom, yet sometimes the fruit of the lofty tree may be reached. From Abu Sa'id I have cast at the smooth slab of rock the noble generous lord and the pure dazzling hero. Among warriors who wear the weave of Saluki armour, while Saluki hunting hounds run with them. They pass the cup of death among themselves in battle, and it is linked to a cup of the finest wine. You have trodden upon the heights of the outposts until you extracted your full due from the fortress. You have lashed them with whips until they were spurred to full gallop on the open plain, driving them lean until they swept every flat plain and every mountain pass. He marched forward into the thick of battle, raising a towering cloud of dust, ascending to the citadel. He was loyal in counsel to the king — the standing king — though kingship is rarely nourished by sincere counsel. Long has it been that obedience to God was drawn from obedience to God's creation. He then settled upon the Byzantine province with his camp, blessed with good fortune and success. He seized its market and left behind a market of death that overwhelmed every marketplace. They fled between the blazing drawn sword and the consuming fire. Finding at the strait what was never found before — neither in that region nor at al-Raziq. No decree of fate could hold him back from his aim save a thin veil of territory between him and it. Had the horses not failed him, nothing of the remote and distant would have remained beyond his reach. A battle that shook the city of Constantinople until it trembled with its breached walls. By the spear he swore an oath sharper than the razor-edged sword. Even had the arm gathered all its strength into the elbow or the arrow been aided at the nock — the enemy would not have seen its gate as they claimed, nor the sea before it as deep. No narrowing in the chest in the hour of terror, no constriction on the morning of the straits. His voice fell silent in the hour of command and prohibition only when it fell low, then the great camel-stallion roared again. How many a prisoner they delighted in, and how many a slain man with his garment soaked in blood like red dye! The patriarch cried for help in ignorance — for who is sought as succour save the one who commands the patriarch? And a captive who saw death until he spoke the truth, though he was no truthful man by habit. He rose to speak to the people with justice — yet the wretched man was far from deserving of justice. He counselled while being no sincere counsellor, and showed compassion while feeling no genuine pity. He maintained religion outwardly while concealing transgression beneath. He sacrificed his all — every horse and warrior — the neighing and braying in his lands stripped of the king's possessions. He sold them neither as adults among them nor did he sever the bonds of hearts through separation. Then he struggled against men over the spoils, and against others with blows and burning. The distance between them and the righteous was like the distance between the foolish and the base. There was nothing between unbelief — had they but reflected — and open iniquity. In the valley of Uqarqas you did not turn away from the trot to battle and the brisk pace. Religion cried out and Islam appealed to you for help as the drowning man appeals for rescue. The day of the Banu Bakr ibn Wa'il with decisive blows surpassed even the day of al-Muhammar the heretic. The day of cutting down locks of hair — that day and this, the day among the Byzantines, the day of cutting throats. The sword fed half of them while the other half was felled by counsel pure in lineage and deep-rooted. They fell silent as if he were hurling them with that strategy from a catapult. By the Lord of the Ancient House, you have toppled from among them the ancient pillar of error. He stripped them of their swords and the poison of his spear-shafts in the nights of the Saruq campaign. Both your raids were honoured — yesterday's and the horsemen's — the affair was no trivial matter. When neither the sky's expanse was green nor the face of winter's day was open and clear, you bequeathed the lesser foe humiliation and shame, and the campaign concluded before dawn. How much land you restored, cold upon the cold — herds of gazelles and a renowned flock of deer. Then you returned, though you feared the heavy cloud, a man of deep thought and a fluttering heart, heeding not the lightning flashes of the white swords. You loathe rain even though it is truly beloved — what wisdom there is in despising what is beloved! You feared not the harm of the enemy nor their transgression, but you feared the harm of the friend. Your glorious days against the Byzantines are red as the morning wine and red as the evening wine — marked as if stained with blood between al-Simak and al-Uyuq. Away from you, O sons of grudges — he is pure of lineage, goodly in character, luminous in the channels of his blood. He does not overlook matters and he does not descend save upon the straight path. Desist, for he who is truly worthy among the people is unfit for that sort of deed. The lofty things belong to him — he meets with nothing but becoming prey to rights. He is wakeful, yet he is of all people the most forgiving toward those whose gifts to him are stolen. I am mad with love for you as long as I live, and intoxicated by you without recovery. My comfort in praise will remain as long as I endure — be content with this blessing which is like a heavenly maiden, neither unloving nor clinging. Her husband may rest assured she will not be disobedient, and she is in a stronghold safe from divorce.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: loud weeping, wailing, sobbing from grief
red wine, specifically the deep crimson variety; also used for the natural redness of cheeks
Byzantine military commander, patriarch or patrician; borrowed from Greek patricius
a prized breeding camel-stallion; used metaphorically for a leader whose voice commands silence and awe
a tall white bird (crane or heron); used as a metaphor for a noble, handsome man
relating to Saluq, a Yemeni town famous for fine coats of mail and hunting dogs
a herd of gazelles or oryx; used as a symbol of beauty and of rich booty

context: A long panegyric qasida in the khafif meter, one of Abu Tammam's most elaborate battle odes. It opens with a nashib lamenting lost loves near al-Aqiq, then transitions into extended praise of Abu Sa'id for his campaigns against Byzantine territories, including a vivid depiction of the siege of Constantinople and the routing of the enemy. The poem concludes with the poet's personal declaration of unending love and gratitude toward his patron.

titleEn: Burning Regrets and Tender Yearning
translationEn: Burning regrets and tender yearnings, and a familiar illness. A tormented heart and flowing tears. The place of meeting is near you, yet it offers no ease. The phantom of your face stands fixed before my eyes, steeped in longing. Wherever I may be, my lord, a vision of you circles around me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pangs of regret, bitter grief, burning yearning
tender feelings, deep affections; also: things that bend or incline toward another
familiar, habitual; an illness that has become a constant companion
streaming, flowing; said of tears that flow continuously
to afford ease, to be convenient or accommodating
a vision or phantom that circles around one at night; a haunting apparition

context: A short ghazal fragment in mujazza al-khafif meter. Abu Tammam distills the essence of longing into five compact verses: regret, illness, a tormented heart, and a phantom image of the beloved that offers no respite regardless of where the lover turns.

titleEn: O Lightning, Rest at the Summit of al-Buraq

verses.ajuz: ئك إن لم تروها من خلاق

verses.sadr: قد سقتني الأيام من يدها سم
يا أبا القاسم المقسم ما بي
لو تطلعت في ودادي إذا فا
ذاك خل جهدت جهدي فلم أح

verses.ajuz: لم تلمني في حب أهل العراق

verses.sadr: ما تمليت مثل ذاك الحجا المع
مع ما قد طويت من سائر النا
verses.ajuz: س وما قد نشرت في الآفاق
دت على الشهد بسطة في المذاق

translationEn: O lightning, spend your night at the heights of al-Buraq, and come down upon it in the morning with a copious downpour — and know that your flashing clouds have no purpose if they do not water it. Ancient ruins where the tears of the weeping clouds used to meet the tears of the lovers, the ruins glistening with that water pouring down, veiled by rain and by the corners of the eyes. May God protect Ismail wherever he has turned, and may a generous leg of rain water him. The days have poured me from their hand a poison — the poison of his loss — in a brimming cup. Perhaps I may in turn take my revenge upon those days without covenant, pledge, or oath. I shall requite on the day of departure, and let no tenderness for the day of parting overtake me. O Abu al-Qasim, you who divide my innermost being — between the membrane of my heart and its outer covering there is none like you. Could you but look into my devotion when it overwhelms you — between my bowels and my collarbones. Brotherhood has grown entangled between us, for true love is a pure root among lineages. That is a friend toward whom I have striven with all my effort and yet cannot measure the full benefit of his intelligence and his company. Could you see how he defends me in my absence, you would not blame me for loving the people of Iraq. I have never been satisfied with the like of that intellect, deep-rooted in forbearance and noble natural qualities, alongside all that it has folded away from other people and all that it has spread throughout the horizons. And those sweet ones so tender in their fingertips — had they given the taste, they would surpass honey in their pleasant flavour; and so soft in their limbs that if they were worn, they would dispense with fine linen. They renew pride whenever a day dawns, while some of them boast in the succession of virtues. He shuns harsh words and vices, knowing that slander of reputations is an enduring shame. When people force him toward that, they find his tongue is bound in shackles. Pure in affection and love in a time when affection has begun to flow in paths other than loyalty. I found true brothers to be the rarest provision of all these provisions, with the brightest face. The two rings of my noose had drawn close, and he loosened with his hands the knot of that strangling. They are shield and breastplate when legs are intertwined on the morning of battle. Had they seen the star of death they would have stretched their necks toward it in eagerness. And an inherited wealth that I did not inherit through lineage, a treasure that is neither gold nor silver leaves.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a place name associated with lightning (برق); the poem opens by addressing the lightning itself
the pericardium, the membrane surrounding the heart; the innermost part of one's being
the collar bones, the clavicles; used to indicate the upper chest and throat
deep intelligence, sound reason and wisdom
stretching their necks forward in eagerness, rushing with fixed gaze
gold; an elevated poetic word for pure gold
a noose, strangulation; used metaphorically for desperate straits

context: A complex qasida in the khafif meter addressed to Abu al-Qasim Ismail. After a conventional rain-prayer opening for beloved ruins, the poem pivots into a warm and philosophically rich eulogy of a close friend named Ismail, praising his intelligence, loyalty, and moral nobility. The final section laments the rarity of true brothers and celebrates the unique treasure of genuine friendship.

titleEn: Rain Drew Near and Lightning Poured Down

verses.sadr: قد لان أكثر ما تريد وبعضه
في الروض قراص وفي سيل الربا
وأخف ما جشم امرؤ وسعى له

translationEn: The rain drew near and that lightning poured down — and the urgent need has parted from you after long companionship. Bravo, O Abu Zayd! Your generosity is wide, your giving is copious, and your glory is towering. Most of what you desire has become easy, and some of it remains rough — yet I am certain of success. In the meadow there are nettles, in the flood of hills there is turbidity, and in some rains there are thunderbolts. I have wed my affair to good fortune, and the ill omens of misery have been thereby divorced from it. The setting places of failure have become, by what you have done, the very rising places of success. My need reached him and he achieved its fullest reach — a noble lord, accomplished in the paths of generosity. Not every man who aims high is the first to do so, nor does every thoroughbred horse gallop ahead of the racing leaders. My need came to him already tried and proven — a mature woman — and I was not pleased in her place to have a mere maiden. It is a calamity that my gratitude should fall silent before what you have done, while your generosity itself speaks out loud. The lightest thing a man can bear and strive toward when he owes thanks to a benefactor is sincere praise. Shall I witness your kindness and then conceal it? I would then indeed be a thief who steals from the hands of the generous.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rain; life-giving rain; also a word used poetically for generosity that gives life
abundantly flowing, wide-spreading; said of generosity or a river
nettles, stinging plants; used to acknowledge that even the best situations have thorns
ill-omened stars or days; misfortunes and bad luck
a noble lord, a chief; literally a camel kept for stud — only the most distinguished
a young woman just reaching puberty, a maiden; contrasted with the mature and proven
a kindness done, a favour bestowed; the act of benefaction

context: A madih qasida in the kamil meter addressed to Abu Zayd. Abu Tammam opens with a rain metaphor to signal the arrival of needed relief, then praises his patron for fulfilling the poet's request. The poem is notable for its extended metaphor of the poet's need as a mature woman finally brought to her rightful home, and for its self-reproach at the inadequacy of mere thanks.

titleAr: يا قضيبا لا يدانيه من الإنس قضيب
titleEn: O Branch No Human Branch Can Match
translationEn: O branch that no branch among humankind can rival. Above it stands the ban-tree and below it a shifting dune sways it. And a gazelle — whenever it passes, hearts long for it. Its cheek is golden, swayed by the blowing breeze. We have not touched him, yet from a single glance he nearly melts.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a slender branch; a common metaphor for a graceful, lithe figure
the ban tree (Moringa peregrina); its branches are a classical metaphor for a slender figure
a sand dune; here the swaying dune beneath the beloved evokes the gentle sway of hips
a young gazelle; the most common classical simile for a beautiful, graceful beloved
the blowing of the wind; a strong or sudden gust

context: A brief and exquisite ghazal fragment in which Abu Tammam sketches the beloved using three classical images — the swaying branch, the shifting dune, and the gazelle — culminating in the paradox of a beauty so overwhelming that a mere glance brings the observer to the verge of dissolution.

titleAr: لم أر شيئا من الفراق اذا
titleEn: Nothing in Parting Is Harder Than Farewell

verses.sadr: لم أر شيئا من الفراق اذا

translationEn: I have seen nothing in separation, when the one parting is a lover overcome with passion, harder than the stance of the one seeing off his beloved, who wishes to say farewell yet must turn away. How beneficial nearness is to the lover, even if his beloved turns away from him and is cold. What lover has ever known complete happiness?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the brother of separation; one who is companion to parting, i.e. someone undergoing separation
deeply infatuated, burdened with passionate love
the one who accompanies a departing person to see them off; the escort at farewell
nearness, physical proximity to the beloved
to be cold and distant, to treat someone harshly; the opposite of warmth in love

context: A short ghazal fragment in the munsarih meter meditating on the acute pain of farewell. Abu Tammam focuses on the particular agony of the one who must watch the beloved depart — the worst moment in the whole experience of separation. The final verse is incomplete in the manuscript.

titleAr: إن يكن في الأرض شيء حسن
titleEn: If Anything on Earth Is Beautiful

verses.sadr: إن يكن في الأرض شيء حسن
verses.ajuz: فهو في دور بني عبد الملك
ما بقي من مالهم أو ما هلك
فهي لا تعرف إلا هو لك

translationEn: If there is anything beautiful on earth, it is found in the homes of the Banu Abd al-Malik. They care nothing, when they give, about what remains of their wealth or what perishes. Their tongues have been restrained from saying no — they know only the words 'it is yours.' Among them is Musa, a generous and noble man who sees nothing he possesses that he has not given away. They have adorned the earth just as the horizons of the sky are adorned with the stars of night.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the sons of Abd al-Malik; a noble family praised by Abu Tammam for their generosity
they gave bountifully, they bestowed grace and favour upon others
restrained, bound, shackled; here their tongues are restrained from refusing
exceedingly generous, open-handed; one who gives freely and fully
noble, glorious, possessed of true majesty and honour

context: A short panegyric in the ramal meter praising the Banu Abd al-Malik — particularly a member named Musa — for their extraordinary generosity. Abu Tammam uses the image of stars adorning the sky as the family adorns the earth, a classic device of cosmic comparison in Arabic panegyric.

titleEn: The Caravan Has Made Me Their Tale

verses.ajuz: وقد كنت في سلم فأصبحت في حرب
أرق وأحفى منك في ساعة الكرب

verses.sadr: متى أتبغى النصف من قلب صاحب
verses.ajuz: إذا لم يكن قلبي شفيقا على قلبي

verses.sadr: فمن مات من حب فإني ميت
verses.ajuz: لئن دام ذا من شدة البغض للحب

translationEn: My ankle-ring rattles — I have become the tale of the caravan, I who was once at peace and have now become embroiled in war. By the life of Amr — even the scorching ground and the blazing fire are gentler and more merciful than you in the hour of distress. When will I seek justice from a companion's heart, if my own heart is not compassionate toward itself? Whoever dies of love — I am that one, if this condition of intense aversion to love should persist.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a tale, a story passed around; something that becomes the talk of people
the caravan riders, a group of travellers riding together
scorching hot sand or ground heated by the sun to burning temperatures
blazing intensely, flaming fiercely; said of fire at its most intense
fairness, justice, equity between two parties
compassionate, tender, genuinely caring for another

context: A brief bitter ghazal in the tawil meter expressing self-reproach and frustration with love. The poet mocks himself — he has become the caravan's gossip — and confronts the paradox of seeking fairness from a beloved's heart while his own heart fails to show mercy to itself.

titleEn: She Scratched Me with Her Palm
translationEn: She scratched me with her palm and gestured with her glance. I gazed at her face, and she shielded herself from me with her palm. Would that my half on the bed could be a blanket for her half. Then I would attain what I desire, despite the tip of her nose.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: she scratched me; a light scratch, often a playful or coquettish gesture
her glance, her eye; the corner of the eye used to gesture coyly
she shielded herself from me, she protected herself using her hand
a blanket, a covering quilt used in bed
despite her displeasure; literally despite the tip of her nose — an idiom for doing something against someone's wishes

context: A playful, intimate ghazal fragment in mujazza al-khafif meter. Abu Tammam captures a flirtatious encounter in four compact verses: a scratch, a glance, a shielding palm, and a wishful fantasy — all rendered with remarkable economy and wit.

titleEn: My Flowing Tears Water Their Dwelling

verses.ajuz: وإن عاد صبحي بعدهم وهو حالك

verses.sadr: سقت ربعهم لا بل سقت منتواهم
إذا الغيث سدى نسجه خلت أنه
verses.ajuz: مضت حقبة حرس له وهو حائك

verses.sadr: أتاكم سليل الغاب في صدر سيفه
إذا سيل سد العذر عن صلب ماله
verses.ajuz: وإن هم لم تسدر عليه المسالك

verses.sadr: هو الحارث الناعي بجيرا وإن يدن
ومستنبط في كل يوم من الغنى
فما تترك الأيام من هو آخذ
verses.ajuz: ولا تأخذ الأيام من هو تارك

verses.sadr: صفوح إذا لم يثلم الصفح حزمه

verses.ajuz: هي المثل في لين بها والأرائك

verses.sadr: غدا وكأن اليوم من حسن وجهه
verses.ajuz: وقد لاح بين البيض والبيض ضاحك

verses.sadr: متى يأتك المقدار ولا تدع هالكا

translationEn: My flowing tears offer hospitality to their dwellings, even as my morning after their departure is darkened. The litters of their departure bore off Zainab and Atika, the most beloved among our dear ones. May rain water their abodes — rather, may it water their new dwelling-place on earth with the heavy udder-clouds. May it clothe them in the embroidered robe of spring and its finery and its Yemeni weave of dew-soaked plants. When the meadow flirted with the sun, its carpets and draping curtains were unfurled in their environs. When the rain stretched out its web, you would think that an era of guards had passed over it as it weaved. Convey my message to the tribe of the Araqim — for from the bird with a burning bowel, gifts are guided home. Eat patience raw and drink it, for you have stirred the camel of oppression and oppression has knelt. The offspring of the thicket has come to you, and in the breast of his sword is a flash against the darkness of oppression — a tearer of injustice. When he is asked, he blocks any excuse for withholding the core of his wealth, and obstacles do not cloud his paths. He rides across the ridges of perilous wastelands, knowing that the lofty goals lie beyond the cliffs of destruction. You pressed and disputed with him, yet fate and piety are two creditors in battle — one who presses and one who disputes. He is al-Harith who mourned Bujayr, and when one draws near to him he is, in his compassion, both Zuhayr and Malik. A shrewd man of war — how often did the dust-columns of battle turn for him into bars of pure silver on the day of terror. And a man who each day digs from wealth a new well, its bucket-ropes fashioned of spear-shafts and horse-hooves. He overlooks his allotted times as if he were a partner to fate in its dealings with souls. Days do not abandon what he takes, nor do they take away what he leaves. He is forgiving when forgiveness does not breach his resolve, and he drives back the reckless killer with a killing stroke. A ward of kings who suckled him at their breast, and the vagrant wanderers heard of the soil of his upbringing. Had he not reined in his cavalry, they would have ground you down with their weight as battle grounds down leather. Had it not been for his piety, he would have shattered like a cracked egg-shell the sheltered women of the curtained chambers. He would have selected the best milch-camels leaving their pregnant nobles wandering with no resting place. You would have worn the shame of an age whose nights, among all nights, are the most wearying and grinding. He would have snatched the mattresses of security from under you — they who are the very byword for softness along with the cushioned couches. But he refused to let your hump among your own people be exposed to his hand, though he was fully capable. He refused to leave you under his shadow while you were the withers of the two tribes of Taghlib and their haunches. Such that the ropes — though braided — would snap, and the bonds of kinship — though intertwined — would be severed. Do not deny the benefits of the silent one — Muhammad — whose flowing gifts in pairs cascade one after another. He directed toward you the south wind of sincerity, a gentle breeze, though it had been blowing obliquely and violently against others. He turned back the thirsty spear from you and the sharp white swords were sheathed still hot. He returned under the best of fortune with his spoils — noble swift horses and patient she-camels. He set forth, and it was as if the day from the beauty of his face, appearing between the white swords and the white shields, was smiling. Your life for the world is a shaded, pleasant life, and your loss for the world is an abiding, devastating extinction. Whenever fate comes for you, let no destroyed one be left — but this is a time that destroys the likes of you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: heavy rain-clouds full of water, with swollen udder-like underside
a clan name; also a type of spotted serpent; a reference to a specific tribe
dust-clouds raised by armies or cavalry on the battlefield
a ward of kings, one raised and nurtured under the care of royal households
wanderers, vagrants, the destitute; in pre-Islamic poetry often raiders who owned nothing
young she-camels, prized for their speed and endurance on long journeys
steady, constant-paced she-camels; those that maintain a regular smooth gait

context: A long tawil qasida opening with a nasib lamenting the departure of Zainab and Atika, followed by an extended rain-prayer, then a substantial panegyric to a patron named Muhammad who has protected a tribe (likely the Banu Taghlib or a related group) from his own wrath. The poem is notable for its vivid battle imagery and its celebration of controlled power — the patron could have destroyed the addressed tribe but chose mercy.

titleAr: تبدلت الفا إذ تبدلت لي ألفا
titleEn: You Changed When You Exchanged My Thousand for Another

verses.sadr: تبدلت الفا إذ تبدلت لي ألفا
verses.ajuz: وقد خانني فيك الزمان وما أوفى
على الرغم مني جرعة مرة صرفا

verses.sadr: رميت بحظي منك في أبعد المدى

verses.ajuz: من الغدر في أجفان عينيك لا تخفى
لعيني تسمو لم أدر لهما طرفا

translationEn: You changed a thousand intimacies when you changed to another for me — time has betrayed me in you and kept no faith. I have made my soul swallow a consolation from your brotherhood — a bitter, undiluted draught, forced against my will. You have grown weary, and weariness is nothing but a grief that one has become so accustomed to that one can no longer divert it. You have flung my share of you to the furthest distance and surrendered it to the wind to scatter it entirely. By God, the flickers of lightning from treachery have never ceased glimmering in the lashes of your eyes, unmistakable. I swear: had I been certain that boredom would come for my eyes, I would never have let them glance once.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you exchanged, you replaced; you traded one thing for another
consolation, the easing of grief; the beginning of forgetting a sorrow
undiluted, pure, unmixed; used of wine or a bitter draught taken straight
weariness, boredom, the gradual cooling of affection toward someone
flashing lights, gleams; here the glints of treachery visible in the beloved's eyes
treachery, betrayal; the breaking of a covenant or promise

context: A tawil ghazal poem of complaint and bitter farewell. Abu Tammam laments a friend or beloved who has grown cold and turned to others, using vivid images of lightning-treachery in the beloved's eyes. The poem is remarkable for its psychological insight — the poet knew all along from the flicker of treachery in those eyes, yet looked anyway.

titleEn: Harun, Best of Those We Hope In
translationEn: Harun, O best of those in whom hope is placed — whoever disobeys you has not obeyed God. Had there been revelation after the Prophet to any guardian, you would have been the one to receive it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: hoped in, depended upon; one to whom people turn with hope and expectation
whoever disobeys you; obedience to the caliph linked to obedience to God
divine revelation; the inspiration given by God to prophets
a guardian, a patron, a close friend of God; a saint or khalifa
the best of those hoped in; superlative praise placing the patron above all as the object of hope

context: A two-verse epigram in the basit meter addressed to the Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid. Abu Tammam distils royal panegyric to its extreme: the first verse equates disobedience to the caliph with disobedience to God; the second verse dares to place Harun at the level of a prophet — the ultimate hyperbole of court poetry, stopping just short of actual prophecy.

titleEn: Imminent Departure and Blazing Longing
translationEn: An imminent departure and an unbinding — and the smoldering heat of longing and burning desire. May my father be ransomed for a beloved I bid farewell, whose company made the companions lose their way. A full moon who illuminates those who love him, untouched by any crescent of waning. Iraq grew distressed and dishevelled in grief at his absence. Death and separation — both of them are unbearable burdens. They conspire together against souls — this one is the death-agony and that one the final departure. Were it not that this thing is as it is, they would never have spoken of death or separation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: distance, remoteness; the state of being far from the beloved
smoldering thirst or passion; an inner burning that is not quenched
the last days of the lunar month when the moon disappears; waning and diminishment
became distressed, turbid and disturbed; used of water or a region overcome with grief
became dishevelled, unkempt; used of one in mourning who neglects appearance
the final death-rattle, the process of the soul leaving the body

context: A mujazza al-kamil ghazal of seven verses about an impending farewell and the twin unbearabilities of death and separation. Abu Tammam draws an unusual philosophical parallel: death and separation cooperate against the soul — one kills the body and the other kills the spirit — and concludes that their names only exist because the reality is so devastating.

titleEn: You Know My Tears and My Longing
translationEn: You are aware of my tears and my yearning, and of the anguish and burning that possess me. And yours is the elegance, the grace, the beauty, the fragrance of the sleeves, and the noble character. It is shameful that my body has shown the very ruins of lovers that I can see all around me. So why this coldness when I have committed no fault? For coldness is separation before separation itself.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a tear; specifically a tear shed from grief or longing
burning anguish of the heart, the acute pain of love's longing
elegance, refined taste, courtly grace in manners and bearing
the sleeves of a garment; their fragrance is a sign of the beloved's beauty and care
turning away, coldness, aloofness; the act of refusing to engage or respond
the falling places of lovers; the ruin and destruction that love brings to the body

context: A khafif ghazal of complaint in four verses. The poet acknowledges the beloved's awareness of his suffering, praises the beloved's many graces, then protests against the undeserved coldness — ending with a memorable epigram: coldness itself is already a form of separation.

titleEn: That Passion and That Fire Have Died
translationEn: That passion and that fire have died, and a gazelle-eyed beloved who is tender toward me has felt compassion. Sleep has begun to flow through my eyelids in the same channel as tears once flowed, and the yearning heart has grown tranquil. Time has been gentle with me concerning my master — and time, when it wills, is a gentle companion to hearts. By my right and my sacred bond — do not wrongfully curse time, for it has been a friend to me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: inner passion, hidden burning grief; intense suppressed emotion
fire, burning; used for the consuming fire of love's longing
felt compassion for me, took pity on me; to be moved by someone's suffering
grew calm, found comfort and familiarity; the heart at ease after long turmoil
my sanctity, my sacred inviolability; invoked as a solemn bond

context: An unusual khafif ghazal in which Abu Tammam celebrates relief from love's torment rather than its perpetuation. Sleep returns, the heart calms, and the poet praises time itself — typically the villain of Arabic love poetry — as his friend and benefactor. The final verse is a surprising reversal of convention.

titleEn: Modesty Silences Me Before You
translationEn: Modesty holds me back from speaking to you — so glances are the messengers between you and me. Our conversation in the innermost soul is in harmony, yet our outward affair in all things is split apart. Our eyebrows convey our secrets to each other, and eyes take aim with the promise of union.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: modesty, shyness; also the redness of twilight — here used for bashful reserve
glances, the pupils of the eyes; used as secret messengers between the lovers
the innermost conscience and soul; the private realm of thought
to convey by suggestion, to signal secretly; a root connected to divine revelation
to aim and shoot; eyes aiming like arrows — a classic image of the wounding glance

context: A three-verse munsarih ghazal of remarkable economy. Abu Tammam captures the entire drama of silent love — modesty prevents speech, so glances become the language; inwardly the lovers are united, outwardly separated; and eyebrows and eyes conduct their own secret conversation of desire.

titleEn: By God, If You Felt What I Feel
translationEn: By God, if you were to experience what I experience, you would find it sinful to go beyond justice. In me is more than what one soul can bear — or do you see it cast aside at your side? She weeps for a wound she inflicts, through a connection that can neither be hoped for nor be healed. Have mercy on a wretch in love with you — he desires nothing, even if you were to set him free.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you would find it sinful, you would be in a state of religious prohibition; to feel the weight of transgression
justice, right, truth; fairness owed between two parties
a wound; a place bitten, gnawed or savaged — used of a wound inflicted by love
to be healed by incantation or charm; to be cured through magic or medicine
a miserable wretch, one destined for suffering; the opposite of the happy and blessed

context: A four-verse kamil ghazal built on the premise of empathy: if the beloved could feel what the lover feels, she would see that continuing her indifference crosses the line of justice. The poem ends with total surrender — even if freed, the lover wants nothing but to remain enslaved.

titleEn: My Father Be Ransom for Your Glance

verses.sadr: ما زلت أرجوك كما لم تزل
والله لو أعطى المنى لم أرد
قد بعدت همة من راح أو

translationEn: My father be ransom for your glance upon your cheek — and your eyes have milked out tears from other eyes. I have never ceased hoping in you, my lord, just as I have never ceased fearing you since the very beginning. By God, if all desires were granted me, I would want nothing but to press your mouth with mine. How low is the aspiration of one who goes through an evening or a morning merely wishing for you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the glance, a sideways look; the corner-of-the-eye glance of the beloved
milked out; as a cow is milked — eyes milking tears from other eyes by their beauty
I feared you; to hold in reverent awe as well as love, fearing the beloved's power
touching or kissing reverently; the act of pressing one's lips to something sacred
high aspiration, ambition, resolve; a man of himma aims for the greatest things

context: A sari' ghazal in four verses that moves from playful praise of the beloved's eyes to a declaration of the single, precise desire of the lover, ending with an ironic observation: to merely wish for the beloved is a sign of low aspiration — true longing demands more.

titleAr: لهف نفسي علي لا بك عليكا
titleEn: I Grieve for Myself, Not for You

verses.sadr: لهف نفسي علي لا بك عليكا
أنت وقف على القلوب بما أص
لا قضى الله لي وصالك إن كن

translationEn: My soul grieves for me — no, more precisely for you — that eyes should roam freely over your cheeks. It is hard for me to bear that gazes should pluck the spring flowers from your two cheeks. You are a pious endowment dedicated to hearts by virtue of your being loved, while those hearts are themselves dedicated to you. May God never grant me your union if I find myself yearning for anyone but you. The evil eye has wounded you with its glances until I have come to fear for you from your very own eyes.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: grief, sighing regret; an exclamation of pain and yearning
the two cheeks; a primary site of beauty description in Arabic love poetry
a pious endowment; property dedicated to a cause that cannot be retracted — here love cannot be revoked
union with the beloved, intimate meeting and closeness
the evil eye; the malignant glance that causes harm to beautiful things

context: A khafif ghazal of five verses notable for its final paradox: the poet fears for the beloved's beauty not from strangers but from the beloved's own eyes — the evil eye typically harms what one gazes upon, so the beloved's own beautiful eyes might harm their own beautiful cheeks.

titleAr: إن حزني علي لا بل عليكا
titleEn: My Sorrow Is for You, Not for Me

verses.sadr: إن حزني علي لا بل عليكا
إن قلبي عليك في كل وصل

translationEn: My grief is for me — no, rather for you — no, for the very soul that flows and melts in your presence. You take pride in a beauty that has drawn all gazes toward it and left them dedicated to you. Cursed be the eye to whose authority the matter was entrusted, and it parted from your cheeks! My father be ransom for your sweet speech, which has left hearing itself obedient in your hands. My heart in your presence — whether in union or in coldness — is more tender than your own cheeks.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the pure life-blood, the soul itself; the innermost vital essence of a person
to take pride in, to glory in; to be beautiful and know it
the eyeball, the eye in its socket; the organ of the gaze
speech, utterance, the words one speaks; here the beloved's sweet words
obedient, docile, compliant; hearing left in willing submission

context: A companion ghazal to the preceding poem (abu-tammam-294), sharing the same rhyme (-iika) and similar themes. The poet circles around the question of for whom his grief is directed — himself, the beloved, or the melting soul — and arrives at the paradox of a heart more delicate than the cheeks it adores.

titleAr: نم فإن لم أنم كراي كراكا
titleEn: Sleep, for If I Cannot Sleep My Sleep Is Yours

verses.sadr: نم فإن لم أنم كراي كراكا

verses.ajuz: نفس مثلي عن أن تكون فداكا

verses.sadr: في سبيل الهوى فؤادي وما آ

verses.ajuz: غير أني أبكي لأن لا أراكا

translationEn: Sleep — for if I cannot sleep, my sleep is your sleep; let that be witness from you that so it is. My suffering has grown long — may my soul ransom you — yet I said: a soul like mine is too low to be your ransom. My heart is on the path of love, and I have no grief over it — only over your memory. My two eyes have gone with their blood and tears — I care not for the parting with the world, but I weep not for the loss of my eyes for the sake of my eyes — only because I will no longer be able to see you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: slumber, sleep; particularly the light sleep of the night-watcher
may something be your ransom; an expression of deep love offering one's life in exchange
the path of love; the road one travels in passionate devotion
the eyeball; the eye itself rather than merely the gaze
for the sake of my eyes; the beloved's face is the reason the eyes have value

context: A khafif ghazal of striking emotional logic: the sleepless lover offers his sleep to the beloved, offers his soul as ransom yet finds it too low a price, and finally weeps not for the loss of his eyes themselves but only because without them he cannot see the beloved. The poem contains a notable OCR dislocation in the fourth verse.

titleAr: يا أبا جعفر اقر لك الحس
titleEn: O Abu Jafar, Beauty Bows to You

verses.sadr: يا أبا جعفر اقر لك الحس
يا أبا جعفر هل النأي ينجي

translationEn: O Abu Jafar, beauty has acknowledged you and its armies have encamped in your shadow. O Abu Jafar, you were created wondrous — the beauty of your nape surpasses the beauty of all faces. O Abu Jafar, does distance bring salvation from you? Far from it — it only increases one's destruction. O Abu Jafar, grant me your union — may God reward you as you deserve if you do.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: acknowledged, confessed, submitted; beauty itself admits the beloved's supremacy
wonderful, unprecedented; created in a fashion never seen before
the nape of the neck; remarkably, Abu Tammam says even the back of the head is more beautiful than all other faces
far from it! impossible! an exclamation of strong negation
union with the beloved; the sought-after intimacy of lover and loved one

context: A four-verse khafif ghazal structured as an anaphora — each verse begins with 'O Abu Jafar' — creating a litany-like effect of address, praise, complaint, and petition. The poem is notable for its comic hyperbole: even the beloved's nape surpasses all other faces in beauty.

titleEn: My Comfort Is in Weeping Until I See You

verses.ajuz: إن لي منك شاغلا عن سواكا
لست أدري ما حيلتي في رضاكا

translationEn: My comfort is in weeping until I see you — for I have in you a preoccupation that keeps me from all others. May rejection be wretched — and he who is more wretched than all people, except you. Guide me to your pleasure, for I do not know what means I have to win your approval. When they ask 'whom do you love?' my tongue stumbles — yet you are that very one in my heart.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may he be wretched, stumble and fall; a curse of misfortune
abandonment, estrangement, the beloved's refusal to engage
guide me, show me the right path; a plea for direction
approval, satisfaction, being pleased with someone
my tongue stumbled, erred, failed to speak correctly

context: A khafif ghazal of four verses in which the poet finds comfort only in weeping, curses rejection, begs for guidance to win favor, and ends with a beautiful paradox: when asked who he loves, his tongue stumbles even though the beloved is enshrined in his heart. The name cannot be spoken though the love cannot be concealed.

titleEn: I Am Stripped of Love Unless I Punish My Eyes
translationEn: I would be stripped of love and cured of it — if only I did not punish my own two eyes. I sent you as a scout, and you stole from him his beauties with a single glance. You came back saying 'I did not see him' — yet his beauties shine visibly upon your cheeks. If you, O messenger, kept him concealed from me, then his beauties have manifested themselves openly upon you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I was stripped bare, left naked; deprived of something that covered and protected
a scout, a vanguard; one sent ahead to explore and report back
beauties, fine qualities; the attractive features of a face or character
the eye, the pupil; the organ of sight and the seat of the glance
to appear clearly, to shine; visible on the surface for all to see

context: A wafir ghazal of conceit and wit. The poet sends his eyes as scouts to observe the beloved, but they return having stolen the beloved's beauty which now shines visibly on the eyes themselves — betraying the lie that they never saw him. A masterpiece of playful self-reproach directed at one's own senses.

titleEn: A King Who Oppresses When He Rules
translationEn: A king who oppresses when he rules — he feels no pity for those who perish. Your beloved's hand tore away the veil of my consolation, and it was rent asunder. O little king — when your slave weeps in love, he laughs! My grief matches in measure the wondrous beauty you possess.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to oppress, to act tyrannically; to exceed just limits
to feel compassion or pity for; to mourn and show sympathy
consolation, forgetfulness of grief; the easing comfort of forgetting
was torn apart, was exposed; the veil was forcibly removed
a little king, a diminutive sovereign; an affectionate or ironic form of malik

context: A four-verse mujazza al-khafif ghazal with a witty political metaphor: the beloved is a tyrant-king who rules without mercy. The poet's grief is proportionate to the beloved's beauty — the greater the beauty, the greater the suffering. The image of a king laughing at his weeping slave is a pointed commentary on the cruelty inherent in beauty.

titleEn: Longing Has Kept Me Company Since You Left
translationEn: Longing has kept me company since your departure — and a tear that strikes in the evening or comes at dawn. They have never gathered at once for my eyes — and since you left my gaze recoils from it — can the narcissus and the rose ever truly unite?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: kept me company, made me feel less alone; to provide companionship in solitude
intense longing, passionate yearning; the overpowering emotion of love's absence
a tear; a single drop of weeping caused by grief
strikes, visits; like a night visitor who arrives unexpectedly
the narcissus and the rose; classical metaphors for black eyes and red cheeks — two beauties that cannot coexist

context: A three-verse sari' ghazal, fragmentary and affected by OCR dislocations in the source. The poem uses the image of the narcissus and the rose — classical symbols of dark eyes and rosy cheeks — to suggest that the two aspects of the beloved's beauty are incompatible: they cannot be present simultaneously for the bereft lover.

titleEn: The Envious Found Something to Say About Us

verses.sadr: عجبوا أن قانصا بث في الآ

translationEn: The envious have found occasion to speak about us — they have aimed arrows and darts at us. They marveled that a hunter spread his snares in the horizons and caught a gazelle who fills my eyes with grace and beauty and my heart with awe and majesty. So blame me for him however you wish and say: God has spared the believers the need to fight!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the envious ones, those who begrudge the good fortune of others
they aimed, they notched an arrow; to prepare an arrow at the notch for shooting
snares, traps; nets spread by a hunter to catch prey
grace, charm, elegance; the quality that makes the beloved irresistibly attractive
awe-inspiring presence, dignity that commands reverence and respect

context: A khafif ghazal in which Abu Tammam addresses the envious gossipers who criticize his love. He reframes his successful capture of the beloved as a hunter's triumph, filling the poet's eyes with beauty and heart with awe — then dismisses the critics with a Quranic phrase (Q. 33:25), applying a divine verse about battle to his personal love affair with characteristic wit.

titleEn: A Gazelle Whose Rose Dwells in His Cheek

verses.sadr: ما كنت أحسب أن لي مستمتعا

translationEn: A gazelle whose rose dwells in his cheek — a cheek upon which fine shirts of rose-petal are laid. I never imagined I had any enjoyment in his nearness until I was afflicted by his distance. Nothing is more beautiful than him on the night of our union, when I have made a pillow of his cheek. And my mouth on his mouth, conversing with his saliva while my hand strolls through the gardens of his skin.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fine inner garments, silken shirts worn next to the skin; here petals of rose on the cheek
enjoyment, benefit, pleasure taken from something
a pillow, a cushion; here the beloved's cheek used as a pillow
to converse intimately at night; to pass the night in close conversation
the gardens of his skin; the body of the beloved imagined as beautiful gardens to wander through

context: A sensual ghazal in the anonymous meter of the source. Abu Tammam describes a night of union with the beloved, using a series of unusually physical and intimate images: the cheek as pillow, the saliva as conversation partner, and the skin as gardens. One of his more erotic short poems.

titleEn: How Long Drags My Endless Night

verses.sadr: يا غافلا عني ما لي أرى

verses.ajuz: في النوم من كثرة ما نقتل

translationEn: How endless drags my longest night! How ceaselessly races my streaming tear! O length of rejection that has no end — you have a reproach that has no beginning. O one heedless of me — why do I see your glance never heedless of killing me? I see you cannot stop being alarmed in your sleep from the sheer number we are slain.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: drags on, extends beyond all limits; to persist far beyond what is bearable
flowing freely downward, pouring in streams; tears that pour without pause
estrangement, rejection, the deliberate refusal to engage
heedless, inattentive, neglectful; paying no attention to the lover's suffering
a sudden alarm, a start of terror; being jolted awake by fright in the night

context: A sari' ghazal of four verses built on the rhetorical device of paradox and reversal. The beloved is called heedless of the poet yet the beloved's own glance is the instrument of killing; and the final verse turns it into dark comedy: the beloved wakes in alarm at night because so many lovers have been slain.

titleAr: شد ما استنزلتك من ربعك الأظ
titleEn: How Deeply the Ruins Drew You Down

verses.sadr: شد ما استنزلتك من ربعك الأظ

translationEn: How deeply the abandoned ruins drew you down from your dwelling until the tears of the gazelle-eyed poured down. What beauty among those who have departed and gone — and what grace upon the backs of their riding camels. And coquetry tenting itself at the summits of the pavilions, and an anklet-wearing woman tormented behind the curtains. And oryx-eyed women from the seclusion of the chambers — gazelles set running toward their fates. A vision visited you on the night of the sandhill from a sandy place between the sacred enclosure and the place of long waiting. Sleep — for no phantom visited you; rather, you yourself journeyed to the phantom of the imagination.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the howdahs and litters of departing women; the caravan of loved ones leaving
coquetry, playful allure; the deliberately charming behavior of the beloved
an ankle bracelet, a jingling anklet; its sound and sight mark the beloved's presence
curtained chambers, the screened enclosures where women dwell
white oryx; plural of maha — a classical metaphor for beautiful wide-eyed women
the phantom of imagination; the dream-vision of the beloved that visits the sleepless lover

context: A khafif nasib poem of six verses evoking the departure of beautiful women from their encampments. The final verse delivers a philosophical inversion: the lover did not receive a dream-visit from the beloved's phantom — rather, the lover's longing soul traveled out to meet it. A meditation on the dream-vision (tayf al-khayal) convention in Arabic poetry.
